Facts.text: Battle of Lake Trasimene: Carthaginian general Hannibal defeats a Roman army headed by Consul Gaius Flaminius during the Second Punic War; Flaminius is killed
Godomar, King of the Burgundians, defeats the Franks at the Battle of Vézeronce
Battle of Thielt (Axpoel): William Clito of Normandy defeats the forces of Thierry of Alsace
Jews are expelled from Nuremberg, Bavaria, by Emperor Maximillian
Battle of Landriano: Imperial-Spanish forces under Don Antonio de Leyva, Duke of Terranova, beat a French army under Francis de Bourbon, Comte de St. Pol
Great fire in Moscow, a third of the largely wooden city destroyed and 2-3,000 killed
Garrison under Adrian of Swieten conquers Gouda
1st Protestant Episcopal parish in America established at Jamestown
Russia and Sweden sign the Peace Treaty of Cardis, ending the Russo-Swedish War
Raid of the Medway: Dutch Admiral Michiel de Ruyter occupies the town of Sheerness in England

In Montreal, New France (Quebec), black slave Marie-Joseph Angélique, having been convicted of the arson that destroyed much of the city, is tortured and hanged by the French authorities in a public ceremony
Pieter Steyn installed as pension advisor of Netherlands
Town of Halifax, Nova Scotia, is founded by the British - sparks Father Le Loutre's War
The first medical diploma in America is granted to Dr. John Archer by the College of Philadelphia
The population of Vizille, France, demands local parliament
British George Vancouver meets Spanish ships Sutil and Mexicana off Vancouver, British Columbia
Great Stone Face, or the Profile found in New Hampshire
Peninsular War: Battle of Vitoria results in a victory for a Spanish, Portuguese and British alliance against the French
African Methodist Episcopal Zion (AMEZ) Church organizes (NYC)
Slave revolt leaders Denmark Vesey and Peter Poyas arrested in South Carolina
Greek War of Independence: Egyptian forces capture Psara in the Aegean Sea
Maniots defeat Egyptians under Ibrahim Pasha in the Battle of Vergas
Battle at Waghausel: Prussian troops beat Baden rebels
Envelope-folding machine patented by Russell Hawes in Worcester, Massachusetts
First Victoria Cross won during the bombardment of Bomarsund in the Åland Islands during the Crimean War
Battle at Upperville, Virginia, Union forces hold off Confederate advance, 389 causalities
New Zealand Wars: The Tauranga Campaign ends
Wagner's opera "Meistersinger von Nuernberg" premieres in Munich
The Molly Maguires, ten Irish immigrants, are hanged at the Schuylkill County and Carbon County, Pennsylvania prisons
Infielder William White plays one game for the Providence Grays with conjecture was the first African-American to play MLB even though he appeared Caucasian
First Ferris wheel opens at the Columbian Exposition in Chicago, Illinois
Workers in Pittsburgh strike Pullman sleeping car company
British Earl of Rosebery's Liberal Party government falls
US captures Guam from Spain during the Spanish–American War
Dodgers score 7 in top of 11th to go ahead of Phillies, 20-13, In bottom of 11th Phillies stalls so umpire forfeits game to Dodgers

In the Philippines, General Arthur McArthur, US military governor of the Philippines, issues an amnesty proclamation to those Filipinos who will renounce the insurgent movement and accept US sovereignty
Boston Herald tells of Red Sox trade "Dougherty as a Yankee," 1st known reference to NY club as Yankees (became Yankees in 1913)
Polish workers in Łódź, Congress Poland stage strikes and insurrection against Russian occupation; quashed by tsars' troops after a few days
E. W. Scripps founds United Press Associations in the US
Tiny Broadwick is the first woman to parachute from an airplane
Anti-British revolt in South Africa ends with arrest of General De Law
The U.S. Supreme Court hands down its decision in Guinn v. United States 238 US 347 1915, striking down an Oklahoma law denying the right to vote to some citizens
Boston Red Sox pitcher Rube Foster no-hits New York Yankees, 2-0 at Fenway Park
Mexican troops defeat US expeditionary force under General Pershing
Hawaiian Red Cross forms
Social Democrat Gustav Bauer forms a German government
The UK, the Dominions, and India, become the British Commonwealth of Nations
NCRV, Dutch Christian Radio Society, forms
First Great Lakes to Gulf of Mexico barge trip is completed in New Orleans
Baseball's Pinky Higgins gets 12th straight hit
German occupiers disband Dutch States-General/Council of State
The first successful west-to-east navigation of Northwest Passage begins at Vancouver, British Columbia
2nd French troops occupies Damascus, Syria
129°F (54°C), Tirat Zevi, Israel (Asian record)
Anneliese Seinheuer throws female world record spear (47.24m)
Federal troops put down racial riot in Detroit; 30 dead
Very heavy bombing on Berlin
US defeat Japanese forces on Okinawa
10 die in fire at Baker Hotel, Dallas, Texas
Federal judge in Seattle rules that club doesn't have to play returning serviceman
1st stored computer program runs on Manchester Mark I at a laboratory in Manchester University, England
HMT Empire Windrush with the first 800 emigrants from the West Indies to the UK arrives at Port of Tilbury near London
Rhodes conference on Israeli-Arab war opens
WNAC (now WHDH) TV channel 7 in Boston, MA (CBS) begins broadcasting
Philippine School of Commerce, through a republic act, is converted to Philippine College of Commerce; later to be the Polytechnic University of the Philippines
Australian middle distance runner John Landy runs world record mile (3:58.0) and 1500m (3:41.8+) in Turku, Finland
Anti-protons detected in the atmosphere
German DR frees almost 19,000 prisoners
Oriole Connie Johnson beats WS Jack Harshman (1-0) in dual 1 hitters
Ellen Louks Fairclough is sworn in as Canada's first woman Cabinet Minister
French franc devalues
German sprinter Armin Hary runs world record 100m (10.0)
USAF Major Robert M. White takes X-15 to 75,190 m
Three civil rights workers, Michael Schwerner, Andrew Goodman and James E. Chaney, disappear after being released from a Mississippi jail, later found murdered by members of the Ku Klux Klan
Yanks take 5-3 lead in 11th & lose 6-5, in 2nd game Red Sox lead 3-2 in 9th & Yanks beat them 6-3
1st reported hearing of pirate radio WGHP (With God's Help Peace)
John Pennel pole vaults world record (5.45 m)
Zager & Evans release "In the Year 2525"
Detroit's Cesar Gutierrez goes 7-for-7 to tie a record set in 1892
International Court of Justice asks South Africa to pull out of Namibia
MLB Cleveland Indians Ken Harrelson retires from baseball to play pro golf
ICC Men's Cricket ODI World Cup, Lord's: West Indies beat Australia by 17 runs to win inaugural final; Player of the match:Clive Lloyd(WI) 102 (85)
Italian General Election; Enrico Berlinguer's Communist party win their greatest-ever share of the vote (34.4%)

The British Army shoots dead 3 Provisional Irish Republican Army volunteers and a passing Ulster Volunteer Force member at a postal depot on Ballysillan Road, Belfast; it is claimed that the PIRA volunteers were about to launch a bomb attack
12-bottle case of 1979 Napamedoc Cabernet wine auctioned for $24,000
Bread riots in Casablanca, Morocco, kill 66 people (government figure vs opposition estimate of 637)
Socialists and communists win French parliamentary election
Stephanie Kay Ashmore, of Alabama, 18, crowned America's Junior Miss
American, Brazilian, and West German forensic pathologists confirm skeletal remains exhumed in Brazil were Nazi Dr Josef Mengele
"Living on Video" by Trans-X peaks at #61
"Vienna Calling" by Falco peaks at #18
Heike Drechsler of East Germany long jumps 7.45 m (women's record)
Discovery rolls over from OPF to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida)
American rock band The Rascals begin their 1st tour in 20 years
Carlton Fisk becomes AL catcher HR hitter, 307th at Yankee stadium
Supreme Court rules ok to burn US flag as a political expression
7.7 Manjil-Rudbar Earthquake with hundreds of aftershocks hits Iran, killing between 35,000 and 50,000 people
At Yankee Stadium rally, Nelson Mandela dons a NY Yankee baseball cap and proclaims "I am a Yankee!"
NYC's Zodiac killer shoots 4th victim, Larry Parham (30), who survives
Parliaments of West Germany and East Germany recognize Oder-Neissegrens
US House of Representatives votes 254-177 to stop US flag burning, doesn't pass
Jirg Haider resigns as premier of Austrian province of Karinthia
STS-57 (Endeavour) launches into orbit
Women's National Basketball Association begins as NY Liberty beats LA Sparks
"Don't Be Cruel" second studio album by Bobby Brown is released (Grammy Award Best Male R&B Vocal Performance, 1990)
Section 28 (outlawing the 'promotion' of homosexuality in the United Kingdom) is repealed in Scotland with a 99 to 17 vote
A federal grand jury in Alexandria, Virginia, indicts 13 Saudis and a Lebanese in the 1996 bombing of the Khobar Towers in Saudi Arabia that killed 19 American servicemen
Total solar eclipse in Madagascar and the Indian Ocean lasts for 4 minutes 56 seconds
Disney animated movie "Lilo & Stitch" released, written and directed by Chris Sanders and Dean DeBlois
SpaceShipOne becomes the first privately funded spaceplane to achieve spaceflight
Pluto's newly discovered moons are officially named Nix and Hydra
ICC Men's Cricket T20 World Cup, Lord's, London: Pakistan wins their first title beating Sri Lanka by 8 wickets; Player of the Series: Sri Lankan all-rounder Tillakaratne Dilshan (317 runs)
ICC Women's Cricket T20 World Cup, Lord's: England 86/4 win inaugural event; beat New Zealand 85; England's Katherine Brunt takes 3 for 6
A boat of 200 asylum seekers headed to Australia capsizes and 110 survivors are rescued
Moody's downgrades 15 major banks in the UK, US, Canada and Europe
15 people are killed and 20 are injured after a suicide bomber attacks a mosque in Peshawar, Pakistan
Qhapaq Ñan, the Andean Road System of the Inca Empire, 30,000km long (18,600 miles) through six countries, granted World Heritage status by UNESCO
Hackers ground 1400 passengers by attacking IT system at Warsaw Chopin airport in Poland
Ninety-four people die and forty-five are hospitalized after drinking moonshine in Mumbai, India
In landmark case Israeli woman wins sexism case against airline El Al after asked to change seat away from a man
Romanian Prime Minister Sorin Grindeanu outed after losing no-confidence vote 241-7
EU imposes tariffs on US goods worth $3.2 billion in response to US tariffs
NBA Draft: Arizona center Deandre Ayton first pick by Phoenix Suns
New Zealand Prime Minister Jacinda Arden becomes the second world leader to give birth in office, to a daughter
NHL Draft: US NTDP center Jack Hughes first pick by New Jersey Devils
New archaeological discovery announced near Stonehenge of a large circle of shafts surrounding a village 2500 BC, largest prehistoric structure in Britain
Saudi Arabia bans international visitors from making the Islamic Hajj pilgrimage in 2020 due to COVID-19
WHO records a new record number of new daily cases of COVID-19 - 183,020, with 116,000 coming from North and South America
Carl Nassib becomes the first openly gay player in the NFL in a post on Instagram
Colombia's COVID-19 recorded death toll passes 100,000, the tenth country in the world to do so
New Zealand weightlifter Laurel Hubbard is the first openly transgender athlete to be selected for the Olympics
Swedish government of Prime Minister Stefan Lofven toppled after a no confidence vote for the first time in Swedish history
Law enforcement's response to the Uvalde school shooting was an "abject failure" says Texas's public safety chief Steven McCraw in testimony at a Senate hearing
Severe flooding affects southern China, including Guangdong and Jiangxi provinces, with areas experiencing their highest rainfall since 1961, forcing authorities to evacuate tens of thousands
South Korea successfully launches its first satellites into orbit with its homegrown rocket Nuri, from Naro Space Center
US approves chicken made from animal cells - the country's first lab-grown meat, aimed at reducing harm to animals and the environment
Landmark case in Namibia rules colonial laws banning same-sex between men are unconstitutional
Ronaldo, a 6-foot Brazilian rainbow boa constrictor, gives birth to 14 babies in Portsmouth, England, despite no contact with another snake, by parthenogenesis (asexual reproduction)
The Netherlands returns 119 sculptures from the Benin Bronzes collection that were looted from the Kingdom of Benin (present-day Nigeria) by colonial forces
Roman GeneralVespasianconquers Jericho during the Great Jewish Revolt
Külüg Khan is enthroned as Emperor of China and seventh Great Khan after defeating rival factions and succeeding his uncleTemür Khan
Queen of England,Catherine of Aragon, speaks against her marriage's annulment at the Blackfriars Legatine Court
Honnō-ji Incident: assassination ofOda Nobunagaby his own generalAkechi Mitsuhidein Kyoto, Japan
French troops under KingLouis XIVoccupy Utrecht
Grand Pensionary of HollandJohan de Wittis seriously wounded by a knife-wielding assassin
Fleeing French KingLouis XVIand family captured at Varennes-en-Argonne
American inventor and businessmanCyrus McCormickpatents the reaping machine
Louisiana chess prodigyPaul Morphyarrives in Europe
William James'the Father of American Psychology' passes his medical examination at Harvard Medical School
Britain celebrates the golden jubilee ofQueen Victoria
The US Republican Party nominatesTheodore Rooseveltfor President, but not without opposition from those whom he calls 'malefactors of great wealth'
Marcus Garveysentenced to 5 years for using mail to defraud
German championMax Schmelingloses NYSAC, NBA and lineal heavyweight boxing titles in controversial split points decision to American Jack Sharkey in NYC, New York
French People's Popular Front government of SocialistLéon Blumfalls
NY Yankees announceLou Gehrig's retirement after doctors reveal he has amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
Germany's Panzer Army led byErwin Rommeltakes Tobruk in Libya, North Africa
US PresidentFranklin D. Rooseveltand British PMWinston Churchillarrive at Hyde Park for the hastily convened Second Washington Conference
Bill Veeckbuys MLB baseball team Cleveland Indians for $2.2 million
Lord Mountbattenresigns as Governor General of India (formerly the last Viceroy)
Joe DiMaggiogets his 2,000th hit, a 7th-inning single off Chick Pieretti, in a 8-2 victory over Cleveland
Louis Braille's remains are transferred to the Panthéon in Paris, while his hands are kept as relics at Coupvray cemetery
Johnny Cashdebuts Top 10 country song "Cry! Cry! Cry!"
John Diefenbaker(C) takes office as Prime Minister of Canada
Louis St. Laurentresigns as Prime Minister of Canada, ending the longest uninterrupted run at the federal level in Canadian history
Cardinal Montini elected PopePaul VI, 262nd head of Roman Catholic Church, succeeding John XXIII
Byron De La Beckwitharrested for the murder of civil rights activist Medger Evers, found guilty 30 yr later
Phillies pitcherJim Bunninghurls a perfect game as Philadelphia beats the New York Mets, 6-0 on Fathers Day at Shea Stadium

"Who's Afraid ofVirginia Woolf?", film directorial debut ofMike Nichols, based onEdward Albee's 1962 play of the same name, starringElizabeth TaylorandRichard Burton, is released (Academy Awards Best Actress, 1967)
Queen Julianaopens the Coen Tunnel in Amsterdam
US Supreme Court Chief JusticeEarl Warrenannounces he will resign once a successor is found
Dmitri Shostakovich's 14th Symphony premieres in Moscow
FIFA World Cup Final, Estadio Azteca, Mexico City: Brazil and Pelé become the first team and player to win the World Cup three times, defeating Italy 4-1 in front of 107,412 spectators
50,000 attend Celebration of Life, rock concert in McCrea, Louisiana; performers includedChuck Berry; Stephen Stills; WAR; John Sebastian; and Delaney and Bonnie
British rock guitaristRitchie Blackmorequits Deep Purple, forms Rainbow
Bülent Ecevitforms a minority government in Turkey which lasts only 1 month
Former White House chief of staffH. R. Haldemanenters prison
Menachem Begin(Likud) becomes Israel's 6th Prime Minister
Andrew Lloyd WebberandTim Rice's musical "Evita," starring Elaine Paige, premieres at the Prince Edward Theatre in London
Donald FagenandWalter Beckerdisband their rock groupSteely Dan
Paul McCartneyreleases single "Take It Away"
Tennis aceArthur Asheundergoes double bypass heart surgery
1985 Heisman Trophy winnerBo Jackson, signs 3-year contract to play baseball with the Kansas City Royals
President Reagangives speech defending his judicial appointments
Mike Tysonsexually harasses a parking lot attendant
Many killed during demonstration against Burmese DictatorNe Winin Rangoon
MLB New York Yankees trade outfielderRickey Hendersonto Oakland A's for eric Plunk, Greg Cadaret, and Luis Polonia
Little Richard[Penniman] gets a star on Hollywood's Walk Of Fame
New York IslandersDenis PotvinandMike Bossyare elected to the Hockey Hall of Fame
P. V. Narasimha Raobecomes Prime Minister of India as head of a Congress Party minority government
English mathematician Andrew Wiles proves the last theorem of French mathematicianPierre de Fermatafter 356 years, solving the world's most difficult math problem
German tennis superstarSteffi Grafbecomes first defending champion to lose in the 1st round of a major tournament (Wimbledon to American Lorrie McNeal)
NHL Draft: Sault Ste. Marie Greyhounds (OHL) centerJoe Thorntonfirst pick by Boston Bruins
US Senator Daniel K. Inouye and 19 other Japanese-American WWII veterans of the 442nd Regiment belatedly awarded the Medal of Honor by PresidentBill Clinton
Mexican artistFrida Kahlois the 1st Hispanic woman to be honored on a US postage stamp
"Harry Potter and the Order of the Phoenix" the 5th book of the series byJ. K. Rowlingis published worldwide in English
Billy Corganreleases "TheFutureEmbrace", his first solo album
"Transformers: The Last Knight," starringMark Wahlbergand directed byMichael Bay, is released in the US and goes on to gross $605 million worldwide
Sara Netanyahu, wife of Israeli Prime MinisterBenjamin Netanyahu, is charged with fraud
UK police called to house of leader contenderBoris Johnsonover alleged altercation with his girlfriend
Kurt Cobain's guitar duringNirvana's MTV Unplugged show sells for a record $6 million
LA jury findsBill Cosbyliable for sexual assault of a 16 year-old in 1975 at the Playboy mansion, awards victim 500,000
MLB Los Angeles Angels designated hitterShohei Ohtanihas career high 8 RBI in 11-inning 12-11 loss to Kansas City Royals in Anaheim, California
Foundation stone for London's St. Paul’s Cathedral is laid
US Constitution comes into effect when New Hampshire is the 9th state to ratify it
Frank Winfield Woolworthopens his first successful "Woolworth's Great Five Cent Store" on North Queen Street, Lancaster, Pennsylvania
The German Navy, feeling betrayed by the terms of the Versailles Treaty, scuttles most of its ships interned at Great Britain's Scapa Flow Naval base in the Orkney Islands

Columbia Records unveils the 33-1/3 rpm LP phonograph record, invented byPeter Carl Goldmark, allowing up to 20 minutes per side (available in 10 and 12-inch diameters) at the Waldorf-Astoria Hotel, NYC; over the next decade, its popularity and profitability push the 78 rpm record out of production
John Hinckley, Jr is found not guilty by reason of insanity of 1981 attempted assassination of US PresidentRonald Reagan
Pope Leo IX [Bruno, Graf von Egisheim und Dagsburg], Catholic Church's 151st pope (1049-54), born in Egisheim, Alsace, Upper Lorraine, France (d. 1054)
Bolesław V the Chaste, High Duke of Poland (1243-79), born in Stary Korczyn, Busko, Poland (d. 1279)
Maria of Austria, Holy Roman Empress (1564-76), born in Madrid, Spain (d. 1603)
Giovanni Del Turco, Italian composer, born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1647)
Increase Mather, New England Puritan minister, born in Dorchester, Massachusetts (d. 1723)
Abraham Mignon, Dutch still life painter, born in Frankfurt, Germany (d. 1679)
Maria Francisca of Savoy, Queen consort of Portugal, born in Hôtel de Nemours, Paris, France (d. 1683)
Cajetan Kolberer, composer, born in Salzburg, Austria (d. 1732)
John Dollond, British optician and owner of 1st patent for achromatic lens, born in London, England (d. 1761)
Luc Urbain de Bouëxic, comte de Guichen, French admiral, born in Fougères, Ille-et-Vilaine, France (d. 1790)
Bendix Friedrich Zinck, Danish organist and composer, born in Schwabstedt, Duchy of Schleswig (d. 1799)
Motoori Norinaga, Japanese physician, scholar, and writer (Kojiki-den (Commentaries on the Kojiki)), born in Matsuzaka, Japan (d. 1801)
Johann Christoph Friedrich Bach, composer and 5th son of Johann Sebastian Bach, born in Leipzig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1795)
Hendrik van Wijn, Dutch histrorian and archivist (Algemeen Rijksarchief), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1831)
Pierre-Nicolas Beauvallet, French artist, born in Le Havre, France (d. 1818)
Alexander J. Dallas, American statesman and financier, born in Kingston, Jamaica (d. 1817)
Pierre-Paul Royer-Collard, French statesman and philosopher, born in Sompuis, France (d. 1845)
Sidney Smith, British admiral, born in Westminster, London (d. 1840)
Emanuel count de las Cases, French historian (recorded last conversations of Napoleon on St Helena), born in Revel, France (d. 1842)
Daniel D. Tompkins, American politician, 6th US Vice President (1817-25), born in Scarsdale, New York (d. 1825)
Siméon-Denis Poisson, French mathematician and physicist (the Poisson distribution), born in Pithiviers, France (d. 1840)
Pyotr Kotlyarevsky, Russian general, born in Olkhovatsky District, Russia (d. 1852)
Princess Augusta of Bavaria, born in Strasbourg (d. 1851)
Wilhelm Speyer, German violinist and composer, born in Offenbach, Landgraviate of Hesse-Darmstadt, Holy Roman Empire, (d. 1878)
Robert Napier, Scottish marine engineer, born in Dumbarton, Dunbartonshire, Scotland (d. 1876)
Wolfgang Menzel, German writer, born in Waldenburg, Silesia (d. 1873)
Charles Thomas Jackson, American physician and geologist, born in Plymouth, Massachusetts (d. 1880)
Karl Friedrich Curschmann, German composer, born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1841)
Carlo Matteucci, Italian physicist and neurophysiologist (bioelectricity), born in Forlì, Papal States (d. 1868)
Félix Danjou, French church organist, arranger, music teacher, organ builder, and political journalist, born in Paris, France (d. 1866)
James Brewerton Ricketts, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in New York City (d. 1887)
Ernst II, Duke of Saxon-Coburg-Gotha (1844-93) and composer, born in Ehrenburg Palace, Coburg, Duchy of Saxe-Coburg-Saalfeld (d. 1893)
Joseph Abel Haskin, American Brigadier General (Union Army), born in Troy, New York (d. 1874)
Edward Elmer Potter, American Brevet Major General (Union Army), born in New York City (d. 1889)
Jean Chacornac, French astronomer, born in Lyon, France (d. 1873)
William Stubbs, English historian and Anglican bishop of Oxford, born in High Street, Knaresborough, England (d. 1901)
Joseph Rainey, American politician, 1st African American in US House of Representatives (1870-1879), born in Georgetown, South Carolina (d. 1887)
Joaquim Maria Machado de Assis, Brazilian writer (Epitaph of a small winner), born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 1908)
John Decatur Barry, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Wilmington, North Carolina (d. 1867)
Ernest F. Cambier, Belgian colonial official (built railway in the  Congo), born in Ath, Belgium (d. 1909)
Daniel Carter Beard, American author, social reformer and founder of the Boy Scouts of America, born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1941)
Henry Ossawa Tanner, American artist, born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania (d. 1937)
Damrong Rajanubhab, Thai prince, son of King Mongkut and brother of King Chulalongkorn and historian, born in Bangkok, Thailand (d. 1943)
Henry Holden Huss, American classical pianist, composer, and educator, born in Newark, New Jersey (d. 1953)
Johannes Schlaf, German writer and translator, born in Querfurt, Germany (d. 1941)
Max Wolf, German astronomer, pioneer in the field of astrophotography, born in Heidelberg, Germany (d. 1932)
Heinrich Wölfflin, Swiss art historian, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1945)
Herbert Brewer, English composer, organist (Gloucester Cathedral, 1896-1928), and choral director, born in Gloucester, England (d. 1928)
Lena Rice, Irish tennis player (Wimbledon 1890), born in Marlhill, Ireland (d. 1907)
Matt Kilroy, American baseball pitcher (MLB single-season record 513 strikeouts 1886; MLB no-hitter 1886; Baltimore Orioles), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1940)
Edwin Stephen Goodrich, English zoologist, born in Weston-super-Mare, Somerset, England (d. 1946)
Clara Immerwahr, German chemist, first woman to be awarded a doctorate in chemistry in Germany, born in Polkendorf, Silesia Province, Kingdom of Prussia (d. 1915)
H. M. Tomlinson, British writer (Sea & Jungle), born in London (d. 1958)
Willem Hendrik Keesom, Dutch physicist (helium vast), born in Texel, Netherlands (d. 1956)
Umberto Brunelleschi, Italian cartoonist and illustrator (Candide), born in Montemurlo, Italy (d. 1949)
Arnold Gesell, American psychologist and pediatrician, born in Alma, Wisconsin (d. 1961)
Josiah Stamp, 1st Baron Stamp, British civil servant, industrialist, economist, statistician and banker, born in Hampstead, London (d. 1941)
Lluís Companys, Spanish politician, 123rd President of the Generalitat of Catalonia (1933-40), born in El Tarròs, Urgell, Catalonia, Spain (d. 1940)
Rockwell Kent, American artist, painter and illustrator (Canterbury Tales), born in Tarrytown, New York (d. 1971)
Feodor Gladkov, Russian socialist writer (Cement), born in Bolshaya Chernavka, Saratov Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1958)
Claude Auchinleck, British Army commander during the Second World War, born in Aldershot, Hampshire, England (d. 1981)
Sir Gordon Lowe, British tennis player, Member of Parliament (Australasian C'ship 1915), born in Edgbaston, England (d. 1972)
Norman L. Bowen, Canadian petrologist, born in Kingston, Ontario, Canada (d. 1956)
Ralph Craig, American athlete (Olympic gold 100/200m 1912), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1972)
Frank Sherman Land, founder of the Order of DeMolay, born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1959)
Hermann Scherchen, German conductor (Winterthur Symphony Orchestra, 1922-50), and music theorist (Lehrbuch des Dirigierens - Treatise on Conducting), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 1966)
Pier Luigi Nervi, Italian engineer and architect (Nuove Struttura), born in Sondrio, Italy (d. 1979)
Alois Hába, Czech composer, born in Vizovice, Zlín Region, Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1973)
Milward Kennedy, British public servant and mystery writer, born in England (d. 1968)
Charles Momsen, American inventor (submarine rescue device), born in Flushing, New York (d. 1967)
Dorothy Stickney, American actress (And So They Were Married), born in Dickinson, North Dakota (d. 1998)
Donald C. Peattie, American botanist and writer, born in Chicago (d. 1964)
Pavel Haas, Czech Jewish composer killed during the Holocaust, born in Brno, Moravia, Austria-Hungary (d. 1944)
Gunnar Ek, Swedish composer and musician, born in Asarum Blekinge, Sweden (d. 1981)
Wilhelm Maler, German composer, born in Heidelberg, Germany (d. 1976)
Al Hirschfeld, American caricaturist (NINA, NY Times), born in St Louis, Missouri (d. 2003)
Louis Krasner, Ukrainian-American violinist, born in Cherkasy, Ukraine, Russian Empire (d. 1995)
Jacques Goddet, French sports journalist and publisher (Tour de France), born in Paris (d. 2000)
Harold Spina, American popular song composer ("Annie Doesn't Live Here Anymore"), born in New York City (d. 1997)
Helene Costello, American dancer and actress (Love Toy), born in New York City (d. 1957)
Lluís Maria Millet, Catalan composer, and musical director (Catalan Orfeón, 1945-77), born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 1990)
William Shea, American lawyer (instrumental in NY Mets establishment; Shea Stadium), born in New York City (d. 1991)
William Frankena, American philosopher, born in Manhattan, Montana (d. 1994)
Kurt Schwaen, German classical  composer, and pedagouge, born in Katowice, German Empire (d. 2007)
Aleksandr Tvardovsky, Russian poet and editor in chief (Novyj Mir), born in Zagorye, Russian Empire (d. 1971) [OS=June 8]
Béla Tardos, Hungarian composer (A beke napja alatt (Under the Sun of Peace), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 1966)
Charles Jones, Canadian contemporary classical composer and music educator, born in Tamworth, Ontario (d. 1997)
Mary McCarthy, American novelist (Group), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 1989)
Irving Shulman, American author and screenwriter, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1995)
Booker Collins, American jazz bassist, valve trombone and tuba player (Mary Lou Williams and Her Kansas City Seven), born in Roswell, New Mexico (d. 1985)
Jan Decadt, Flemish pianist and composer, born in Ypres, Belgium (d. 1995)
William Vickrey, Canadian economist (Nobel 1996), born in Victoria, British Columbia, Canada (d. 1996)
Buddy O'Connor, Canadian Hockey HOF center (Stanley Cup 1944, 46 Montreal Canadiens; Hart Memorial Trophy & Lady Byng Memorial Trophy 1948), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 1977)
Joe Bamford, British manufacturer and multi-millionaire, born in Uttoxeter, Staffordshire, England (d. 2001)
Eddie Lopat, American baseball player (NY Yankees), born in New York City (d. 1992)
James Bysse Joll, British historian, born in Bristol, England (d. 1994)
Gérard Pelletier, French Canadian journalist, politician, and diplomat, born in Victoriaville, Canada (d. 1997)
Paolo Soleri, Italian-American architect, born in Turin, Italy (d. 2013)
Helen Cattanach, British Army Nursing Service (QARANC), born in Knockando, Morayshire, Scotland (d. 1994)
Frank Scott, American pianist (Lawrence Welk Show), born in Fargo, North Dakota (d. 1995)
Jane Russell, American actress and leading sex symbol in 1940s-50s (The Outlaw), born in Bemidji, Minnesota (d. 2011)
Jean de Broglie, French politician, born in Paris (d. 1976)
Jean Kent [Joan Summerfield], British actress (Caravan, Bond Street), born in London, England (d. 2013)
